mnemonics.title: PRIM'S STEPS
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER PRIM'S WITH: SELECTING MINIMUM KEY REPEATEDLY UPDATES ADJACENT NEIGHBORS - ALWAYS GROW FROM SMALLEST EDGE

mnemonics.title: MST EDGE COUNT
mnemonics.content: VERTICES MINUS ONE EQUALS MST EDGES - ALWAYS REMEMBER V-1

mnemonics.title: COMPLEXITY COMPARISON
mnemonics.content: DENSE USE ARRAY (V SQUARED), SPARSE USE HEAP (E LOG V)

acronyms.expansion: Minimum Spanning Tree - connects all vertices with minimum total weight
acronyms.usage: Prim's Algorithm outputs an MST for a connected weighted graph

acronyms.expansion: PRiority based MST Algorithm - uses priority queue to select minimum edges
acronyms.usage: Name of the greedy MST algorithm

acronyms.expansion: Number of Vertices in the graph
acronyms.usage: Used in time complexity O(V²) or O(E log V)

acronyms.expansion: Number of Edges in the graph

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a tree starting from one vertex and growing branches outward, always choosing the shortest branch available. Like a vine growing across a garden, picking the nearest unconnected plant at each step.
Imagine cutting the graph into two halves. The minimum bridge (edge) connecting these halves is always part of the MST. Think of building a bridge across a river at the narrowest point.
MST is like a skeleton connecting all cities with minimum total road length. No detours needed - straight line connections only.

analogies.analogy: Building a railway network connecting multiple cities: Prim's Algorithm is like starting from one city and gradually adding the shortest railway tracks to connect new cities, ensuring minimum total track length.
Installing telephone lines between houses: Prim's Algorithm finds the most cost-effective way to connect all houses without redundant connections, like laying wires along minimum total distance.
A kingdom expanding its borders: At each step, claim the nearest unclaimed territory (minimum weight edge), growing the kingdom systematically until all territories are united.
